title: Chemistry Foundations
description: This course teaches the language, measurements, and core principles of chemistry before students study atoms, molecules, and reactions.

title: Atomic Structure
description: This course explains **what atoms are made of**, how electrons are arranged, and how modern atomic theory developed. It is the foundation of all chemistry.

title: Periodic Table
description: This course explains **why the Periodic Table is organized the way it is** and how it predicts the physical and chemical behavior of every element.

title: Chemical Bonding
description: This is one of the most important chemistry courses. It explains **why atoms bond, how molecules form, and why substances have different physical and chemical properties**.

title: States of Matter
description: This course explains **why matter exists as solids, liquids, gases, and plasma**, and how temperature, pressure, and intermolecular forces determine their behavior.

title: Thermochemistry
description: This course studies **how energy changes during chemical reactions**. It is one of the core subjects of physical chemistry and explains why reactions release or absorb heat.

title: Chemical Equilibrium
description: Chemical Equilibrium explains **why most chemical reactions never go to completion** and how reaction conditions affect the balance between reactants and products.

title: Acids & Bases
description: This course studies **acid-base chemistry**, one of the most important areas of chemistry. It explains pH, neutralization, buffers, titrations, and the behavior of acids and bases in biological, environmental, and industrial systems.

title: Electrochemistry
description: This course studies **the relationship between chemical reactions and electricity**. It forms the foundation of batteries, fuel cells, electrolysis, corrosion, and modern energy storage.

title: Chemical Kinetics
description: This course studies **how fast chemical reactions occur, what controls their speed, and how reactions proceed step by step**. It is fundamental to chemistry, biology, pharmaceuticals, and chemical engineering.

title: Main Group Chemistry
description: This course studies the **chemistry of the main group elements (Groups 1, 2, and 13–18)**, including their properties, compounds, reactions, and industrial importance.

title: Transition Metals
description: This course studies the **d-block and f-block elements**, their unique properties, variable oxidation states, colored compounds, magnetic behavior, catalytic activity, and industrial importance.

title: Coordination Chemistry
description: This course studies **coordination compounds (metal complexes)**—molecules in which a central metal atom or ion is surrounded by ligands. It is a major branch of inorganic chemistry with applications in medicine, catalysis, biology, and materials science.

title: Organic Chemistry I
description: This course introduces the **chemistry of carbon compounds**. Students learn how organic molecules are structured, named, classified, and how they react. It forms the foundation for pharmaceuticals, biochemistry, polymers, and materials science.

title: Organic Chemistry II
description: This course builds on Organic Chemistry I by exploring the **chemistry of functional groups**, reaction mechanisms, organic synthesis, and spectroscopic identification. It is the core course for pharmaceuticals, medicinal chemistry, and chemical synthesis.

title: Biomolecules
description: This course studies the **chemical molecules of life**. It bridges chemistry and biology, explaining the structure, function, and interactions of carbohydrates, proteins, lipids, nucleic acids, enzymes, vitamins, and biomolecular metabolism.

title: Polymers
description: This course studies **polymers—large molecules made of repeating units (monomers)**. It covers natural and synthetic polymers, polymer synthesis, properties, processing, and applications in materials science, medicine, and engineering.

title: Analytical Chemistry
description: This course studies **how chemists identify, separate, and quantify chemical substances**. It is the foundation of laboratory science, pharmaceuticals, environmental testing, forensic science, and quality control.

title: Spectroscopy
description: This course studies **how light and electromagnetic radiation interact with matter** to identify atoms, molecules, and materials. It is one of the most important tools in chemistry, biology, medicine, and materials science.

title: Environmental Chemistry
description: This course studies **the chemistry of Earth's environment**, including the atmosphere, water, soil, pollution, climate change, and sustainable chemical practices. It connects chemistry with ecology, public health, and environmental engineering.

title: Materials Chemistry
description: This course studies **how the composition, structure, and bonding of materials determine their properties and applications**. It bridges chemistry, physics, engineering, and nanotechnology.

title: Nanochemistry
description: This course studies **chemistry at the nanoscale (1–100 nm)**, where materials exhibit unique physical, chemical, optical, electrical, and biological properties. It is foundational to nanotechnology, medicine, electronics, and advanced materials.

title: Nuclear Chemistry
description: This course studies **the chemistry of atomic nuclei**, radioactive materials, nuclear reactions, and their applications in medicine, energy, industry, and scientific research.

title: Industrial Chemistry
description: This course studies **how chemistry is applied in large-scale manufacturing**. It covers chemical process industries, industrial production methods, process optimization, quality control, sustainability, and safety.

title: Computational Chemistry
description: This course studies **how computers are used to model, simulate, predict, and design chemical systems**. It combines chemistry, mathematics, physics, computer science, and artificial intelligence to solve problems that are difficult or impossible to study experimentally.
